attributes.stem:
A 73-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with his son due to increasing falls and memory difficulties over the past 18 months. His son reports that his father has become increasingly withdrawn and has fallen backwards several times. His walking has become slow and unsteady. He has difficulty maintaining eye contact during conversations and appears to have lost interest in his usual activities.

His medical history includes hypertension, type 2 diabetes and depression. His medications include metformin, gliclazide and ramipril. He lives with his wife and previously managed all activities of daily living independently.

On examination, he appears withdrawn but oriented. His pulse rate is 72/min, blood pressure 138/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.7°C. His gait is slow and broad-based with a tendency to fall backwards. Neurological examination reveals marked limitation of vertical eye movements, particularly downward gaze. There is axial rigidity with extension of the neck, and he has difficulty initiating speech. Mini-Mental State Examination score is 24/30 with deficits in executive function.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Referral to memory and falls clinic
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with clinical features highly suggestive of progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP), including vertical gaze palsy (particularly downgaze), early falls (especially backwards), axial rigidity, and cognitive decline. These symptoms have progressed relatively rapidly over 18 months, which is typical for PSP.

Referral to movement disorders clinic or similar multidisciplinary team service is the most appropriate next step as it would facilitate comprehensive assessment, diagnosis confirmation, and initiation of appropriate management strategies. A movement disorders specialist can coordinate the multidisciplinary care needed, including physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech therapy, while monitoring disease progression and managing complications.

SSRI therapy would not address the underlying neurological condition. While the patient appears withdrawn, this is likely a manifestation of PSP rather than primary depression, and SSRIs have not been shown to improve outcomes in PSP.

Trial of levodopa therapy may be considered but typically provides only modest benefit in PSP, unlike in Parkinson's disease. The poor response to levodopa is actually a distinguishing feature of PSP. However, a trial may still be worthwhile as some patients show partial response.

Referral to memory and falls clinic alone would be insufficient as it would not address the multiple aspects of PSP requiring management, particularly the movement disorder and falls risk.

Anticholinergic medication would be inappropriate as it could worsen cognitive function and increase the risk of falls, particularly in an elderly patient.

The management of PSP is primarily supportive and focuses on maintaining function and quality of life. Regular monitoring for complications such as aspiration, falls, and cognitive decline is essential. Family education and support are also crucial components of care.
learningPoints: Progressive supranuclear palsy (PSP) is characterised by vertical gaze palsy, postural instability, and cognitive decline.
Multidisciplinary care including physiotherapy, occupational therapy and speech therapy is essential in PSP management.
Levodopa trial may provide modest benefit in some PSP patients but response is typically poor compared to Parkinson's disease.
references: Rowe JB, Holland N, Rittman T. Progressive supranuclear palsy: diagnosis and management. Practical Neurology. 2021 Oct 1;21(5):376-83. Available online at ; https://pn.bmj.com/content/practneurol/21/5/376.full.pdf
